Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time each day, but it rarely worked out the way I planned. I'd often end up studying in bursts when anxiety kicked in about upcoming deadlines. With 18 online courses, I felt overwhelmed trying to keep track of everything, so I'd just tackle whatever seemed most urgent at the moment. Sometimes I'd spend hours just figuring out what I needed to do rather than actually doing it.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, logging in daily and watching videos. Other weeks, I'd barely touch anything until panic set in about an assignment. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but life kept getting in the way and my anxiety made it hard to stick to plans.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or that nagging feeling of guilt when I hadn't checked in for a few days. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety worse. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me to log in more often because there was something satisfying about completing those challenges. But mostly it was fear of falling behind that pushed me to check the LMS.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking social media. The gamified elements helped with that because I wanted to see my progress and earn points. But that routine fell apart whenever I got overwhelmed or stressed about other things in life. It's hard to maintain habits when your mental state is all over the place.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the content at my own pace. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. The gamified tasks made even boring content feel more manageable because there was a reward element to completing them.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming and I just couldn't bring myself to sit through pages of text when I was already struggling to keep up. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. The discussion posts I was supposed to respond to also piled up because engaging with others felt exhausting when I was anxious.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or progress. The gamified tasks were great for this because I could see my points adding up. If something directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd prioritize it. But if it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't affect my grade, I'd push it aside even if it might have helped me learn.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my anxiety already high about required work, adding optional stuff felt impossible. Required activities had this weight to them that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. Optional ones? I'd look at them, think 'that seems interesting,' and then never touch them because something more urgent was always waiting.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to study offline, especially when I needed to review without the distraction of the internet. I also used YouTube videos and other websites to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well enough for me. My handwritten notes were a huge part of my studying that the LMS would never capture.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my downloaded notes or rewatching saved videos without logging into the LMS at all. I'd also study with classmates over video calls, discussing concepts and working through problems together. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Sometimes my best studying happened completely disconnected from the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline when I was trying to really understand something. The same with PDFs and slides. Once I had them saved, I preferred studying from my laptop without being connected because it helped me focus better and reduced my anxiety about seeing all the other tasks I was behind on.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I had multiple deadlines converging from different courses, and my anxiety spiked to the point where I'd just freeze. I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, feel completely overwhelmed, and then close the laptop. It wasn't that I didn't care—I cared too much and it paralyzed me. My resilience just isn't great when pressure builds up.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in with good intentions but just scroll mindlessly through the LMS without actually absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities like family stuff and trying to maintain some semblance of a social life meant the LMS often got the worst version of me. Confusion about what was actually required also made me waste time trying to figure things out instead of learning.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd click through materials to feel productive without actually engaging with them. It was like going through the motions to ease my guilt rather than genuinely learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the ones that felt too big or confusing. My anxiety made starting things incredibly hard because I'd worry about doing them wrong. I knew they were important, but the fear of failure made avoidance feel safer in the moment. It's a terrible cycle because the postponing just made the anxiety worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd barely touch things until they were due soon, then scramble to complete them. The LMS became much more active for me in the days before deadlines. It's not healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could cut through my paralysis and anxiety to actually make me work.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it genuinely lifted my mood and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that course for a while because I felt defeated. The gamified elements gave instant feedback which helped keep me going. Waiting too long for feedback on assignments made me disconnect from that material mentally.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything laid out clearly helped me know what to expect, but seeing 18 courses worth of content made it feel insurmountable. When modules were broken into smaller chunks, I could manage better. But when everything felt interconnected and massive, I'd just shut down.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier because they broke things into achievable goals. Clear deadlines helped too, even if I procrastinated until them. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many different platforms and tools to navigate. Consistency in design would have helped my anxious brain a lot.
Q20: I think that's probably someone like me who logs in with good intentions but can't follow through on the actual work. You might be exploring, trying to understand what's needed, or just seeking comfort in feeling connected to the course without having the energy to produce anything. High anxiety can make you very present in the system but unable to actually complete things.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion wasn't great. I think some people just learn better outside the system and only need minimal LMS interaction to succeed. Or they're efficient and don't need to spend hours on the platform to understand the material. The LMS activity doesn't equal learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if I was actually paying attention or just had a video playing while I stared at the ceiling. They miss all the offline studying, the mental effort, the anxiety that shapes how we interact with everything. Numbers don't capture the human experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being present mentally and emotionally. For me, engagement feels like those rare moments when anxiety quiets down enough for me to actually absorb and think about the content. It's hard to achieve consistently.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected everything I did in the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent worrying about assignments instead of doing them, or the offline studying that actually helped me learn. It can't show the gamified tasks that genuinely improved my mood and motivation, or how peer interactions outside the platform supported my learning. The emotional journey of being a struggling student is invisible to data.
